title: 21. Journey Through a Cosmic Portal: A Serpent Ladder: —Franklin LaVoie
text: 21 JOURNEY THROUGH A COSMIC PORTAL: A SERPENT LADDER FRANKLIN LAVOIE The death and mourning of a beloved friend opened a cosmic portal, which gave rise to hypnogogic trance, wherein I encountered a “serpent ladder.” In May 2005 I was in Tulum, Mexico, for a memorial service to honor the life of Sanna Parker, a community leader, a free-spirit dancer, and a charismatic friend. Some family and friends gathered in Tulum to celebrate Sanna’s life and memory. We participated in the ceremony called “Lifting the Shadow.” I arrived at the Buffalo Airport, May 18, 2005, at around 5:30 a.m. Turns out, I didn’t know my itinerary: no departure time, flight number, connecting city, not even what airline I was flying on. To make matters worse, I had to enter all this information into a computer terminal before I could even approach somebody for assistance. The pressure of not knowing if I was late, coupled with feeling idiotic, plus the computer protocol, all caused emotional turmoil. I felt like the Fool from the Tarot deck (card number 0)—not only did I feel foolish, but my knowledge of my itinerary was zero. But I was steeped in meditation practice, so I observed all this and forgave myself. Under the circumstances, what’s the worst that could happen? I’d have to stay home, and I could live with that. It was a stressful situation for fifteen minutes, until my friends arrived. We got our tickets and were on our way. I’d cut myself some slack and maintained my composure in a stressful situation. I’d passed some kind of emotional trial on the threshold of a cosmic portal. We got to Tulum that afternoon, and the next evening we participated in the first part of the Lifting the Shadow ritual. This timeless ceremony was exquisitely performed by two mystical friends of Sanna’s from Tulum. I appreciated every word and gesture they made. Thirteen candles were lit ceremonially around on a simple cross, bound with flowers, bound with prayers. Three days later we would finish the ceremony: dismantle the altar, take the flowery cross to the sea, and offer prayers on Sanna’s behalf, thanking the universe for her fifty-two-year sojourn with us. Meanwhile, the day after the first part of the ritual, we headed to Playa Paraíso, or Paradise Beach—I and seven of Sanna’s family and friends (all women, except me). I put on a mask, snorkel, and fins and headed for the water. When up to my crotch in water, two things happened simultaneously: four large fish nearly brushed my knees and a voice just like thunder clearly spoke my name. In an instant I dismissed the voice as some sort of auditory hallucination. “Maybe a dump truck grinding its motor,” I thought, assuming my auditory nerves had dredged up my name from memory somehow. But the fish, on the other hand, were plainly real, so I went swimming without turning around. Some minutes later I discovered that I was swimming with my fanny pack on. It contained my wallet, my fancy new camera, my money, and ID, plus my papers for my return to the States. How do you say “crap!” in Mayan? Disturbing as it was, I instantly chose to lighten up on myself. I didn’t piss ‘n’ moan to make matters worse. I quipped that I was “just having a little trouble in Paradise.” I spared myself a lot of grief while my wet items dried. It hadn’t escaped my attention that I was at Playa Paraíso, in “paradise,” and the sun was virtually on the zenith, conjunct the Seven Sisters, and here I was in ceremony with seven women. Meanwhile, I’d just emerged from the sea with my ID “all washed up.” What if I’d turned to identify that thunderous voice? It was like the voice of Chacmool—the local Mayan Rain Spirit. This probably wouldn’t have happened. As it was, I’d negotiated another emotional ordeal, with a meditative heart, drawing nearer to the cosmic portal. My decision to be kind, forgive myself, and lighten up (despite the circumstances) feels paramount to what happened next. On the third day, all the women went to another beach “even nicer,” they reported, than Playa Paraíso. But I chose to stay behind at the cabana, to be alone and meditate. I sat quietly on a hammock meditating. Softly, sea waves stroked the shore. The whole atmosphere was sublime. When, out of the blue, a jaguar stepped into my mind’s eye! (Understand, I was wide awake, with my eyes closed, and yet I was suddenly dreaming vividly.) A large jaguar from my right flank lay down in front of me, with a cat’s air of nonchalance. Although something extraordinary was happening, I continued to observe quietly and follow the breath. I became aware of a white pyramid in the distance. It was flanked by two human figures in the foreground. It seemed that these two were also very intent upon this jaguar. This lasted several minutes, and then suddenly a snake rose up, right in my face. She (for I knew somehow that the snake was female) looked beautiful and wise, yet dangerous; she was sentient, venerable, and immeasurably exquisite. I knew instinctively the serpent was here to swallow me. I was thrilled. I knew I was having an honest-to-God vision. I loved everything about this snake. As I went into her mouth, throat, and neck, swallowed whole, I felt no fear, only thrilling love. Surrendering to her beauty, her wisdom, everything triggered unconditional love. In the darkness of her belly, I thought, as I was dissolving: “love you, love you, love you.” Trusting the process, I dissolved into oblivion. Simultaneously, I was on the beach, wide awake, meditating. I’d passed the final test review of some alchemical mystery play, choreographed in my mind’s eye. Ecstatic wonder took me because of this visionary phenomenon; sitting wide awake, with a full-on hypnogogic dream, I stayed planted, meditating. Minutes later, another vision occurred: I was under the sea, heading east over the reefs. There were two guides, like dolphins, behind me, one on each side. We were heading out, perpendicular to the shore, out to the continental shelf. From there, straight down to the ocean’s abyssal plain, then farther down, into utter darkness and oblivion. Meanwhile, on the beach, I was listening to the waves. And then it struck me that I was right between North and South America, and this seemed to me to be like the two hemispheres of the brain. Central America was like the corpus callosum and the Yucatan was the thalamus or the pineal gland (which help regulate waking and dreaming). The reefs I’d just crossed resembled sulci along the neocortex; the continental shelf was like one side of the fissure of commissure; the abyssal plain was comparable to the dense corpus callosum that spreads below the fissure of commissure. The structures in my brain were being out-pictured as the landscape in this hypnogogic “vision,” while the earth’s geography seemed like a simulacra of the brain’s physiology. The sensation of one’s internal physiology getting projected as the outer landscape is explored in a seminal book, The Body of Myth , by J. Nigro Sansonese, in which yogic meditation practices, proprioception, mythology, and visionary trances are examined at length and in detail. I intuited that other features of the earth’s geography were of a psychological nature: the Yucatan jungle, the Mayan jungle, corresponded with the conscious (rational) world of the senses, or maya . The sea corresponded to the moist unconscious, the magic realm, where dreamtime and archetypal imagination coexist, and where great forces in the spirit occasionally emerge from behind veils, darkness, and the uncharted depths of our ignorance. The shoreline was a liminal territory, where the creatures of the sea sometimes meet the creatures of the land. But the shore was also a primeval boundary where the conscious and the unconscious minds carry on a relationship between their different levels of awareness, linking our perceptions and our feelings, and the waking and dreaming states of consciousness. It’s feasible that the unconscious realm, which we experience in our dreams and in hypnogogic visions, was, once upon a time, not so unconscious. Nowadays, our dreams tend to fade quickly and we generally disregard them. Insofar as the rational mind dominates our waking consciousness, we remain unconscious of the soul’s capacity for clairvoyant hypnogogic dreaming. Recovering the soul’s capacity for hypnogogic dreaming may involve awakening the heart mind. The heart mind is associated with the limbic brain, the emotional cortex. Dreams may be vestiges of an archaic faculty that’s generally dormant now, except during REM sleep. Consider that the pineal gland is a vestigial eyeball, sunk deep in the core of our brains, sheltered from direct light. The pineal gland evolved in fish for perceiving light in the outer world; now, in humans, the pineal gland is a light-transducing organ, it can produce molecules that create incredible visions. The heart mind uses symbols to communicate with the rational mind across the intelligence divide. The rational mind has so dominated our lives that the heart mind recedes into the unconscious, because our survival depends on a sensory viewpoint and rational thought. So the heart-mind, the feeling center, sinks into the dark, watery unconscious, until it’s awakened by circumstances. Death is the great awakener. There are many circumstances that awaken the heart-mind and bring on visionary states; a heart-rending loss is only one catalyst among many emotional states that can awaken the mind’s eye, revealing the otherworld, making it seem tangible. Bent so low by death and separation, the otherworld opens on hinges in the heart. The mythical figure carved on the Temple of the Descending God at the ancient ruins of Tulum is an inverted person, with his legs above and arms below, also known as the “Diving God.” Above this figure appear two plumed serpents, which might symbolize the eclipse cycle, with its “dragon nodes” tracking the cosmic intercourse of the sun and moon; or they might depict the human kundalini circuit, which consists of twin serpents called Ida and Pingala; or they could represent the universal tension of opposites, which produces friction and the “new fire” during the twilight wedding of the ages. Like that figure of the mythical diver, I found myself diving into a cosmic portal—one beyond the reefs, far below the waves, in the darkness and oblivion. There’s a wormhole, or serpent ladder, at the core of the world; it’s accessed in our brain’s core, with the assist of the heart mind. There’s an esoteric function of the psyche that involves a journey to the “afterlife,” in the “otherworld.” There’s a choreography of archetypal figures that occupy a universe centered on a human cosmic fractal. That otherworld is a liminal zone, with entry points to supernatural domains and encounters with great forces in the spirit. Here’s to the otherworld of the mystical heart, and the Perennial Philosophy and sacred geometry. Countless traditions the world over tell of heavens and hells, which are liminal worlds: partly psychological metaphors and partly biocosmic dimensions, or otherworlds , each connected to the middle , on earth. Tales of shamans from myriad cultures describe trips to the otherworlds, such as the folk legends of the Eskimos that tell of journeys to the otherworld on the aurora borealis. Much attention was paid to the otherworld by Carlos Castaneda, which he encountered in lucid dreams. Graham Hancock examines shamanic legends and ancient depictions of the otherworld and compares them to UFO narratives in his book Supernatural . The erudite Terence McKenna waxed poetic about the otherworlds, which he personally explored and recorded. In the philosophy of Siddhi Yoga (the yoga of mental training), visionary states are a given, although adepts are cautioned not to fixate on their otherworldly experiences. The otherworld is entered into through the imaginal realm. The soul encounters the otherworld by opening the heart mind, the feeling nature, which has a unique way of visualizing reality. But it’s submerged in an unconscious state for the most part; therefore, it’s akin to a lost continent, deep within. The point being that visions are not unprecedented and experiences with the otherworlds have been reported by every culture. Meanwhile, back at the cabana, the vision resumed: I was rising to the surface from the dark, watery abyss. All was a paradox: my trajectory was still downward, yet I was coming up out of the sea, which was a buoyant ascent—on the other side, descending feels like ascending. Even stranger, I could make out the world beyond the waves. Illuminated beyond the ocean’s surface was a landscape with a pyramid, as viewed from the sky. I’m rising out of the ocean and descending from the sky simultaneously! I know the otherworld is full of paradoxical situations, and this was certainly one of them. Now I was at ground level, looking at another style of pyramid. It was made of white stones, a step pyramid. I observed architectural features that looked like shapes found in nature: crystals, shells, feathers, and the like. I embraced the temple complex as an archetypal image, like the Heavenly City; its architectural motifs reminded me of cosmic ages and the regeneration of life forms. Seen another way, it was a design template (a temple) about the manifestation of conscious awareness. I later mused that the ruins of Tulum (and many other sacred sites) were an attempt to reproduce the many ineffable qualities of that eternal structure. I instinctively knew that I’d entered Xi Balba Be in the all-pervading galactic core, the Hu Nob Ku, City of White Stones, over whose moonlit walls drape beautiful, shady groves of incense-laden trees, above snow-white moonstones. It’s a very comfortable realm, with unique, symmetrical promenades shaped like the jaguar snout motif. I was aware of people and they were aware of me, but I didn’t see anyone. I was looking for Sanna as I wound through wide marble passages, enjoying the peaceful ambiance of shade trees in the moonlight. I had entered the House of the Dead and of Shadows, the Underworld, Xi Balba Be. Its white walls glowed like lotus petals, the fragrance of floral trees wafted on an imperceptible breeze. My soul had descended on the current of the Ida , drawn down by the full moon’s energy through a wormhole (in the earth—the serpent ladder) to the microcosmic orbit, the compressed core of an all-pervading singularity, the galactic core, via the root chakra. This is where the soul descends and reunites with loved ones and friends after life. It’s not at all hellish, but rather pleasant, like very bright moonlight on white marble terraces, under shady bowers of greenery. During the descent into the abyss, in the emptiness of oblivion, my attention split in two. One part continued descending, and the other ascended, to this: I was looking over a beautiful archipelago at a group of islands in a heavenly sparkling sea. Rising sunbeams splayed their fingers through clouds, framing the rich archipelago, in a glistening haze. I was gazing on the Central Sun, Alcyone, rising behind the isles of Paradise. In the mind’s eye, I saw paradise islands on a shimmering sea. This image coincided with a proprioceptive survey of the ventricles in the brain, made possible by the white flame piercing my heart. It’s love, unselfish love, that sears the heart, causing the soul to rise out of its pain like a phoenix; lifting the spirit all the way to the Paradise Isles in heaven’s crown, above and beyond the noonday sun. This is where her “symphony and song” informed me: “Sanna has the Central Beach of Paradise for her dance ceremonies, which are the best ever.” With these words, like solar flares, the sacred river rose in me and flowed from my eyes “like a mountain stream gushes by both sides of a rock.” Neither sobs nor weeping, but streams and currents of ecstasy and pain through my heart of stone, which now shone white-hot like the sun. At the same time, I was on the beach, breathing along with the waves at high tide, and I was struck by a conundrum: is the Central Beach of Paradise a celestial place? A macrocosmic reality, up there, literally, in the Pleiades, in mid-heaven? Or was it a beautiful memory of Tulum thirty years before, when Sanna was genuinely awakened, right here, and leading dance ceremonies, during the psychedelic 1970s? Or was it only the temples of the mind? Paradoxically, it was all the above. I could now feel Sanna’s spirit as authentically as Tulum, she was enlightened on the shores of Tulum, and this permeated her essence, her sunny spirit, that mercurial soul. Hermes Trismegistus of Hellenistic Egypt refers to the cycle of precession as “the Great Year of the Pleiades.” The Hindus saw the Pleiades as Agni—the God of Fire. 1 The First Fire Ceremony was practiced by Toltec and Mayan priests. The Pleiades were culminating with the sun on the zenith; the phrase “Playo Ides” rolled over and over in my head, Ides —as in the Ides of March —but I pronounced it like the eddies on the beach. It translated the central shore , and this implied the fissure of commissure conjunct the corpus callosum between the temples of the skull, as well as the biocosmic temples (the pyramids) above, and below, in the fractal world of the liminal vision. The central sun in the Pleiades is Alcyone. The homonym of Alcyone is “all cyan,” which calls to mind the Blue Star of Hopi myth. My heart was reflecting the light of the sun on the zenith back up into my skull, illuminating the mind’s eye. I thought I recognized the constellation Gemini as the Hero Twins from the Popol Vuh. I saw them as the personifications of the Ida and Pingala , connecting the root and crown chakras, with the emotional tones of tragedy and comedy. I recognized them to be the guardians of the serpent ladder, which connected Xi Balba Be, at the galactic center, with the Pleiades, culminating on the zenith. The cosmic portal was opened outside by the sun and moon and the constellations. It was opened inside by the liminal figures from the otherworld; they performed the ritual choreography to execute the hypnogogic journey. It was sanctioned through the Lifting the Shadow ceremony. And it was negotiated with the heart-mind techniques of nonaggression toward the self and others. All the players in the vision corresponded with a stellar group. For example, the jaguar as Leo; the guards as Gemini; the serpent as Serpens; the dolphins as Dorado, the dolphin fish. Each constellation depicts an archetype, or great force in the spirit, corresponding to one of the figures in the vision. Leo, as the Sphinx, initiated the trance. Gemini, as two universal forces holding tension, producing current. Serpens Cauda touching galactic center. Dorado, the Dolphin Fish, the south precessional pole. They are mercurial and alchemical signs. Knowing something about them and their symbolic milieu can open a dialogue, offering some insights when they appear in meditation, or out there in the world of appearances. They are transformative symbols of alchemical processes; they exist independent of our ego, on other planes, where aspects of our being coexist elsewhere. The star patterns outside the body are closely associated with a set of myths concerning something going on inside the body-mind. Constellations are signs regulating the human condition. Mythologies are like operating instructions, written by our ancestors, for the likes of us. Myths illuminate the (mostly) invisible and unconscious world, including the liminal and imaginal realms, expressing truth in the language of the right brain—using symbols. Myths, like walnuts, require cracking, they require memorizing, they need a cogitation period, and finally they reveal their insights (it’s as though insight has a literal meaning in the otherworld). It follows, then, that the constellations are like the picture book (think right-brain version) of the operating instructions. Constellations were discovered by our earliest forebearers, before the advent of writing; they’re like messages in a bottle; they are practically eternal patterns. And they serve many species, not only humans. Humans mythologized them. The human condition occupies a mysterious position in the known universe; we are right in the middle of everything, a multifaceted fractal whole, in two parts. Spontaneously, the vision resumed: I was inside the biocosmic temple. The same pyramid that I’d seen from the outside, but I was inside now. It was composed of geometric tiers of glowing spheres, exquisitely arranged in concentric rings or squares. Between the spheres were deep geometric nests, marked by more glowing orbs. I study the patterns, trying to memorize them. While I was concentrating, I became aware of a vibration that rose and fell, like the tide; it rode up and lay back down. It went into a chant: “Pull-lay-at-ease.” Play-at-ease. Pleiades! It was a droning sound, buzzing like a bee, while I slowly rematerialized back into my fully conscious body. My whole being felt like I was vibrating at a very natural frequency, perfectly “attuned” with the ocean, in tune with the sky, in tune with electron shells of love in all directions, fully at peace, deeply at ease. My heart of stone now felt like the sun shining inside. Relief is corporeal. The Lifting the Shadow ceremony succeeded in lifting my shadow. It’s clear to me now that Sanna’s soul and spirit have achieved their sublime destinations: her soul’s back home in heaven; and she’s free to dance in Paradise. Sanna was a radical emissary of light. She returned to the biocosmic temple, a beloved celebrity now. This vision in Tulum was a transpersonal experience. It was much more than a daydream. It felt like I’d discovered a lost continent beneath the sea; an otherworld, previously hidden from view. I believe the jaguar that I encountered in the vision was the inspiration for the Sphinx of Giza. (I agree with John Anthony West that the Sphinx originally had the head of a lion.) I think the jaguar represents the heart mind, the limbic system, as it appeared in the liminal world, a Leo body. It was as though the jaguar colluded to distract the twins, who were guarding the serpent, which came out of the pyramid-temple to swallow me whole. After the serpent swallowed me, my attention was relocated to the underworld and the mid-heaven. Theoretically, the mammalian brain held the attention of the frontal lobes, allowing the (serpentine) sensory nervous system to operate (without the constraints of the frontal lobes) as a ladder, so the perceiver (in the spirit and the soul) could visit the upper and lower worlds. The circumstances of Sanna’s life and death, and the Lifting the Shadow ceremony, awakened the heart mind, revealing a clairvoyance of an otherworld and the afterlife. The impression that autonomous “great forces in the spirit” dwell on the other side of a veil and may never appear to the waking mind’s eye, gives me pause and I wonder: What else am I not seeing? What other mythical, legendary, or unknown wonders have simply chosen not to step out from behind the veil of ignorance? A good grasp of archetypal psychology, alchemy, and metaphysics provides a foundation for knowing the hypnogogic experience intelligently, for reducing things psychologically when necessary, and for seeing things as transpersonal and therefore with less personal morbidity. So much of the world is upset and transforming quickly. The rational mind may not find all the answers. To integrate the extraordinary gifts of the soul, we can practice meditating, concentrating on awakening the heart mind, focusing attention inward for the shift. This may help humanity find many practical solutions that are simply not available to the externalized, rational mind. I believe this vision in Tulum can add to our understanding of the human condition and ancient, esoteric philosophy and iconography, which empirical science can neither prove nor disprove. All the best spiritual traditions encourage us to seek the truth, to meditate, to open our hearts, and to have patience. In the current climate of anxiety, climate change, civil unrest, and imbalances beyond measure, we can achieve balance by cultivating the heart, thereby developing an awareness of the liminal realm and cultivating conditions for a hypnogogic vision, wherein sacred forces in the spirit may appear to awaken the heart. Sanna’s many gifts in life were overshadowed only by this journey to the afterlife, through a cosmic portal, and an encounter with a serpent ladder at Samna, near the ancient ruins of Tulum.
